expectations.expectation: Review begins by establishing the scope and context of what artifact is being evaluated
expectations.justification: Opens with 'Artifact Under Review' section: 'A design document for an event-driven notification system that fans out user notifications (email, push, SMS) via a single NotificationRouter service consuming from Kafka. Expected growth from ~10k events/day to ~1M events/day within 6 months.'

expectations.expectation: Identified single Kafka partition with single consumer as the most critical scaling bottleneck, explaining why it cannot handle the 1M events/day growth target
expectations.justification: F1 is the first Fatal Challenge. Calculates that with 600-1500ms per event (3 channels at 200-500ms each), throughput is 0.7-1.7 events/second, failing at ~60k-150k events/day — well short of 1M. Explains that a single Kafka partition cannot be split across consumers and vertical scaling doesn't address the I/O bottleneck.

expectations.expectation: Identified missing retry mechanism and dead-letter queue for failed notifications as a major reliability risk
expectations.justification: F2 classified as Fatal. Quotes the design ('No retries'), calculates ~3,000 lost notifications per day at 0.1% failure rate, explains absence of DLQ means failures are unrecoverable, and proposes a 4-part fix including DLQ topic and delivery status tracking.

expectations.expectation: Identified synchronous fan-out (email, push, SMS in same process) as a risk for latency and cascading failures
expectations.justification: S1 identifies three specific problems: serial execution per event (600-1500ms total), cascading failure ('If Twilio's API slows down... it blocks the entire pipeline — email and push notifications back up behind slow SMS calls'), and no crash isolation. Proposes separate Kafka topics per channel.

expectations.expectation: Identifies that a slow or failing channel (e.g., Twilio SMS timeout) blocks processing of all subsequent events in the queue
expectations.justification: S1 explicitly states: 'If Twilio's API slows down (e.g., 10-second response times), it blocks the entire pipeline — email and push notifications back up behind slow SMS calls, even though SES and Firebase are healthy.' Also: 'A single slow or failing provider degrades or halts all notification delivery across all channels.' This clearly identifies the cascading/blocking effect of synchronous fan-out on event processing.

expectations.expectation: Identified missing rate limiting or backpressure for external providers (SES, Firebase, Twilio) at scale
expectations.justification: S2 cites specific provider rate limits (SES: 14/s default, Twilio: 1 SMS/s per long code, FCM: 500/s), calculates ~35 API calls/second at 1M events/day, and explains that without rate limiting combined with no-retry policy, throttled messages are permanently lost.

expectations.expectation: Flagged the 'scale up' strategy as inadequate for the stated growth from 10k to 1M events/day
expectations.justification: Within F1, directly quotes 'we plan to scale up the instance' and provides three reasons it fails: (1) the bottleneck is I/O wait, not CPU/memory, (2) a single Kafka partition cannot be split across consumers, (3) vertical scaling has a hard ceiling.

expectations.expectation: Every major finding includes a proposed fix or mitigation, not just identification of the problem
expectations.justification: All five major findings include concrete proposed fixes: F1 proposes 12-24 partitions with consumer group partitioned by user_id, F2 proposes per-channel retry with backoff and DLQ topic, S1 proposes separate Kafka topics per channel with independent consumers, S2 proposes per-provider rate limiters with batch APIs and backpressure, S3 proposes table partitioning with async audit writes.

expectations.expectation: Challenges reference specific parts of the design (e.g., 'single instance', 'single Kafka partition', 'no retries') rather than making generic criticisms
expectations.justification: Directly quotes the design throughout: 'The NotificationRouter runs as a single instance consuming from a single Kafka partition', 'If a channel fails, we log the error and move on to the next event. No retries', 'a function call within the NotificationRouter process.' Every challenge is grounded in specific design text.

expectations.expectation: Findings are organized by severity with the most critical issues first and an overall assessment with verdict
expectations.justification: Organized as Fatal Challenges (F1, F2), Significant Challenges (S1, S2, S3), Minor Observations (4 bullet points), and Overall Assessment with verdict ('Has fundamental issues that must be addressed before implementation'), confidence ('High'), and summary.

expectations.expectation: Overall assessment reflects that the design has fundamental issues that must be addressed before implementation
expectations.justification: Verdict is explicitly 'Has fundamental issues that must be addressed before implementation.' Summary: 'its scaling architecture is fundamentally inadequate for the stated growth from 10k to 1M events/day' and the implementation architecture 'needs to be redesigned around horizontal scaling, channel isolation, and resilient delivery.'

eval_feedback: All 11 expectations met. The new blocking-channel expectation is clearly satisfied by S1's explicit Twilio example showing cascading failure across all channels.
